parts.content: Explain how GPP data reflect variations in primary production across biomes, using Image B.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains that $\partial GPP/\partial c_a$ indicates how strongly primary production (GPP) increases as atmospheric $CO_2$ rises <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies evergreen broadleaf forest (EBF) as showing the greatest response/highest values <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes that other forest/woody biomes (e.g. OF, SW) show intermediate responses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Notes that grasslands (GRA) and/or croplands (CRO) show lower responses than EBF <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Based on Image C, explain how declining plant nutrient content could reduce secondary production in food webs.
parts.markscheme:
- States that concentrations of several essential nutrients/minerals decrease at elevated $CO_2$ (e.g. N, P, Fe, Zn), lowering food quality for consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that reduced N (and other nutrients) can reduce protein synthesis in herbivores, slowing growth and reducing biomass gain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that consumers may need to eat more plant biomass to obtain sufficient nutrients, increasing losses via respiration/egestion and reducing net biomass production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links reduced herbivore growth/reproduction to less food/biomass available for higher trophic levels (lower secondary production) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest how this might affect trophic efficiency and biomass transfer in ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggests trophic efficiency would decrease because a smaller proportion of ingested plant material is converted into new consumer biomass when nutrients are limiting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggests more plant material is lost as faeces/undigested material due to low nutrient content, reducing biomass transfer <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the $CO_2$ fertilization effect, although increasing GPP, might contribute to positive feedback in the climate system over time. Refer to at least two of Images A–C.
parts.markscheme:
- Uses Image A and/or B to explain that increased $CO_2$ can increase GPP (positive fertilization response) but the response is not uniform across regions/biomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses Image B to note that some systems (e.g. $C_4$) show a small response, limiting additional carbon uptake at ecosystem/global scales <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses Image C to explain that nutrient dilution/declines (e.g. N, Fe, Zn) can lead to nutrient limitation that constrains sustained biomass accumulation and carbon sequestration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that if carbon uptake saturates/declines while respiration and decomposition continue, net ecosystem carbon storage may not increase and atmospheric $CO_2$ can keep rising <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that increased biomass can increase fuel loads, so drought/heat can increase fire frequency/intensity, releasing stored carbon (positive feedback) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one scenario in which increased plant growth might shift to a net carbon source.
parts.markscheme:
- Describes an environmental stress scenario (e.g. drought/heatwave) that reduces photosynthesis/GPP despite elevated $CO_2$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes that respiration and/or decomposition can remain high (or increase with temperature), so $R > GPP$ and the system becomes a net $CO_2$ source <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes a disturbance scenario such as wildfire, pest outbreak, or land-use change that rapidly releases carbon from biomass/soil, outweighing gains from increased growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links reduced nutrient availability (consistent with Image C) to limited long-term carbon storage and greater turnover of biomass to the atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any other correct, clearly described scenario in which carbon outputs (respiration, decomposition, combustion) exceed carbon inputs (GPP), making net ecosystem production negative <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify the compound that forms when carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater.
parts.markscheme: Carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Recall the carbon cycle
steps.content: To answer this question, we need to recall the chemical interaction between atmospheric gases and the hydrosphere. In the carbon cycle, specifically within **Topic C4.2 (Transfer of energy and matter)**, we learn that carbon dioxide ($ \text{CO}_2 $) is soluble in water. When it dissolves in seawater, it doesn't just sit there as a gas; it undergoes a chemical reaction with the water molecules ($ \text{H}_2\text{O} $).

highlights.text: Identify the compound that forms when carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater

steps.title: Identify the chemical reaction
steps.content:
The reaction between dissolved carbon dioxide and water produces a weak acid. The chemical equation for this process is:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \end{aligned}$$

This compound is the first step in the process of ocean acidification. It later dissociates into hydrogen ions ($ \text{H}^+ $) and hydrogen carbonate ions ($ \text{HCO}_3^- $), which is why the pH of the ocean drops as $ \text{CO}_2 $ levels rise.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on the chemical reaction and the IB Biology syllabus requirements for the carbon cycle, the compound formed is **carbonic acid**.

According to the markscheme, providing either the name or the chemical formula is sufficient for the mark.

Final Answer: **Carbonic acid ($ \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 $)**

steps.title: Identify the reactants
steps.content: To find the identity of the compound, we first look at what is reacting. The question asks what forms when carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) dissolves in seawater, which is primarily water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$). This is a key part of the carbon cycle and ocean acidification, which you can explore further in **Topic C4.2: Transfer of energy and matter**.

highlights.text: Identify the compound that forms when carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater

steps.title: Deduce the chemical formula
steps.content:
When these two inorganic molecules combine, they form a single neutral acid. By balancing the atoms from our reactants, we can deduce the formula of the product:

- **Carbon (C):** 1 from $\text{CO}_2$
- **Hydrogen (H):** 2 from $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Oxygen (O):** 2 from $\text{CO}_2$ + 1 from $\text{H}_2\text{O} = 3$

Putting these together gives us the formula:
$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$$

steps.title: Identify the product
steps.content:
The molecule $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ is known as **carbonic acid**. 

In the context of the provided graph, the formation of this acid is what leads to the decrease in ocean pH (making it more acidic) and the subsequent reduction in available carbonate ions, which corals need to build their skeletons.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, the correct identification is either the name or the chemical formula:

**Carbonic acid** (or $\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$)

parts.content: Compare the pH levels and availability of carbonate ions in the late 1800s and the projected 2100 conditions.
parts.markscheme:
**Similarities:**
- Both time periods show seawater pH values within the range of 7–9 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both conditions show the presence of carbonate ions ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) in seawater <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Both periods show the formation of bicarbonate ions ($\text{HCO}_3^-$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Differences:**
- pH is lower (more acidic) in 2100 compared to the late 1800s <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Carbonate ion ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) availability is lower in 2100 compared to the late 1800s <marks>1 mark</marks>

*[Max 3 Marks]*

steps.title: Identify the comparison task
steps.content:
To answer this question using a variable-based comparison, we will look at two specific variables across the two time periods provided: the late 1800s and the projected 2100 conditions. We need to identify both similarities and differences for:
1. **pH levels**
2. **Carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) availability**

This topic is a key part of **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**, specifically looking at how anthropogenic $CO_2$ emissions lead to ocean acidification.

highlights.text: Compare the pH levels and availability of carbonate ions in the late 1800s and the projected 2100 conditions.

steps.title: Compare pH levels
steps.content:
First, let's look at the pH variable across both time periods:

*   **Similarity:** In both the late 1800s and the year 2100, the seawater remains slightly alkaline, with pH values falling within the range of $7$ to $9$.
*   **Difference:** The pH in the late 1800s is higher (approximately $8.1$), whereas the projected pH for 2100 is significantly lower (approximately $7.7$), meaning the water becomes more acidic over time.

steps.title: Compare carbonate ion availability
steps.content:
Next, we examine the availability of carbonate ions ($CO_3^{2-}$):

*   **Similarity:** In both time periods, carbonate ions are present in the seawater, and both periods show the presence of bicarbonate ions ($HCO_3^-$) as well.
*   **Difference:** There is a clear reduction in the concentration of carbonate ions. The availability of $CO_3^{2-}$ is much lower in the 2100 projection compared to the late 1800s. 

This decrease occurs because the extra $CO_2$ reacts with water to form $H^+$ ions, which then react with $CO_3^{2-}$ to form $HCO_3^-$, effectively "using up" the carbonate ions needed by corals.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the full 3 marks, ensure you state at least one similarity and two differences (or vice versa) clearly:

1.  **Similarity:** Both periods have a pH between $7$ and $9$ (or both contain $CO_3^{2-}$/$HCO_3^-$).
2.  **Difference (pH):** The pH is lower/more acidic in 2100 than in the late 1800s.
3.  **Difference (Ions):** Carbonate ion availability is lower in 2100 than in the late 1800s.

steps.title: Analyze the chemical context
steps.content:
To compare these two time periods, we need to look at the chemical equilibrium shifts shown in the diagram. The **Chemical Species and Reaction Analysis** approach focuses on identifying which molecules are present and how their concentrations change as more $\text{CO}_2$ dissolves into the water.

From the figure, we can identify several key chemical species:
*   **Carbon dioxide** ($\text{CO}_2$)
*   **Bicarbonate ions** ($\text{HCO}_3^-$)
*   **Carbonate ions** ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$)
*   **Hydrogen ions** ($\text{H}^+$), which determine the pH

steps.title: Identify similarities in species
steps.content:
Despite the changes in concentration, the fundamental chemical makeup of the ocean remains similar in both the late 1800s and 2100:

1.  **pH Range**: In both periods, the pH remains in the slightly basic/alkaline range (between 7 and 9). The late 1800s shows a pH of 8.1, and 2100 is projected at 7.8.
2.  **Ion Presence**: Both conditions show the presence of carbonate ions ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) and the formation of bicarbonate ions ($\text{HCO}_3^-$). These species are essential components of the ocean's buffering system.

steps.title: Analyze differences in availability
steps.content:
The main differences occur because increasing atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ (from $280\text{ ppm}$ to $770\text{ ppm}$) shifts the chemical equilibrium:

*   **pH Levels**: The pH is lower (more acidic) in 2100 compared to the late 1800s. As more $\text{CO}_2$ reacts with water, it produces more $\text{H}^+$ ions, dropping the pH from 8.1 to 7.8.
*   **Carbonate Availability**: The availability of carbonate ions ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) is significantly lower in 2100. The diagram shows it dropping from $275\text{ \mu mol/kg}$ to $175\text{ \mu mol/kg}$. This happens because the extra $\text{H}^+$ ions react with carbonate to form more bicarbonate ($\text{HCO}_3^-$), consuming the carbonate needed by organisms.

steps.title: Summary of comparisons
steps.content:
When writing your answer, ensure you provide both similarities and differences to earn the full 3 marks. 

**Similarities:**
*   pH values remain between 7 and 9.
*   Carbonate ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) and bicarbonate ($\text{HCO}_3^-$) ions are present in both periods.

**Differences:**
*   pH is lower in 2100 (7.8) than in the late 1800s (8.1).
*   Carbonate ion availability is lower in 2100 ($175\text{ \mu mol/kg}$) than in the 1800s ($275\text{ \mu mol/kg}$).

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content:
To answer this question effectively, we will use the **Similarity and Difference Categorization** approach. This means we'll first look at what remains consistent across both time periods (similarities) and then identify how the specific values for pH and carbonate ions change (differences).

This topic links to **Topic D4.3 (Climate Change)**, specifically how increased atmospheric $$\text{CO}_2$$ leads to ocean acidification and impacts marine calcifiers.

highlights.text: Compare the pH levels and availability of carbonate ions in the late 1800s and the projected 2100 conditions.

steps.title: Identify shared characteristics
steps.content:
First, let's look at the similarities between the late 1800s and the projected 2100 conditions. Even though the values change, they share certain fundamental traits:

*   **pH Range:** In both time periods, the seawater remains slightly basic (alkaline). If you look at the left y-axis, all pH values from the 1800s to 2100 fall within the range of $7$ to $9$.
*   **Ion Presence:** Both conditions show the presence of carbonate ions ($$\text{CO}_3^{2-}$$) and bicarbonate ions ($$\text{HCO}_3^-$$). The lines for these ions do not disappear; they simply shift in concentration.

Identifying these similarities earns you marks for demonstrating a broad understanding of the chemical state of the ocean.

steps.title: Analyze directional differences
steps.content:
Now, we contrast the two eras by looking at the direction of change shown by the slopes on the graph:

*   **pH Levels:** The pH is significantly **lower** (more acidic) in 2100 compared to the late 1800s. It drops from approximately $8.15$ to about $7.75$.
*   **Carbonate Ion Availability:** The availability of carbonate ions ($$\text{CO}_3^{2-}$$) is **lower** in 2100. On the right y-axis, you can see it drops from over $200\text{ \mu mol/kg}$ to approximately $150\text{ \mu mol/kg}$.

In IB Biology, remember that a decrease in pH is often described as "acidification," even if the solution remains above pH 7!

parts.content: Explain why an increase in atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ causes a reduction in marine carbonate ion concentration.
parts.markscheme:
- $\text{CO}_2$ forms carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$) which releases $\text{H}^+$ ions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\text{H}^+$ reacts with $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ to form $\text{HCO}_3^-$ (bicarbonate) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Carbon dioxide dissolves in water
steps.content:
When there is an **increase in atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$**, more of this gas dissolves into the ocean's surface waters. 

Once dissolved, the carbon dioxide reacts with water molecules to form **carbonic acid** ($H_2CO_3$):

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$$

steps.title: Dissociation into hydrogen ions
steps.content:
Carbonic acid is unstable in water and quickly dissociates (breaks apart). This process releases **hydrogen ions** ($H^+$) and bicarbonate ions into the water:

$$\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$$

This increase in $H^+$ concentration is what lowers the ocean's pH, making it more acidic. Identifying that carbonic acid forms and releases these hydrogen ions earns the first mark.

highlights.text: CO2 forms carbonic acid (H2CO3) which releases H+ ions

steps.title: Consumption of carbonate ions
steps.content:
The free hydrogen ions ($H^+$) produced in the previous step are highly reactive. They readily react with existing **carbonate ions** ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) in the seawater to form more bicarbonate ($\text{HCO}_3^-$):

$$\text{H}^+ + \text{CO}_3^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_3^-$$

Because the carbonate ions are being "used up" by this reaction with hydrogen ions, their overall concentration in the ocean decreases. Explaining this reaction earns the second mark.

highlights.text: H+ reacts with CO32- to form HCO3- (bicarbonate)

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To summarize the sequential process for your exam:
1. Increased $\text{CO}_2$ creates more **carbonic acid**, which releases **$H^+$ ions**.
2. These **$H^+$ ions** react with **carbonate ions** ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$).
3. This leads to a **reduction in marine carbonate ion concentration**.

This is a critical concept in **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**, as it explains why shell-building organisms struggle in more acidic oceans.

highlights.text: reduction in marine carbonate ion concentration

steps.title: Carbon dioxide dissolves
steps.content:
When atmospheric $CO_2$ levels increase, more of the gas dissolves into the ocean's surface waters. Once in the water, $CO_2$ reacts with water molecules ($H_2O$) to form **carbonic acid** ($H_2CO_3$). This is the first step in the chemical equilibrium shift:

$$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3$$

This process is part of the carbon cycle covered in **Topic C4.2: Transfer of energy and matter**.

steps.title: Release of hydrogen ions
steps.content:
Carbonic acid is a weak acid, meaning it partially dissociates (breaks apart) in water. It releases **hydrogen ions** ($H^+$) and bicarbonate ions ($HCO_3^-$):

$$H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$

This increase in $H^+$ ions is what causes ocean acidification (a drop in pH). This step is crucial for earning your first mark.

highlights.text: CO2 forms carbonic acid (H2CO3) which releases H+ ions

steps.title: Shift in carbonate equilibrium
steps.content:
To understand why carbonate ions ($CO_3^{2-}$) decrease, we look at the equilibrium between bicarbonate and carbonate. Under normal conditions, the ocean has a healthy supply of carbonate ions:

$$H^+ + CO_3^{2-} \rightleftharpoons HCO_3^-$$

According to **Le Chatelier's Principle**, if you increase the concentration of a reactant ($H^+$ in this case), the equilibrium will shift to the right to oppose the change. This means the extra $H^+$ ions "consume" the available $CO_3^{2-}$ to produce more bicarbonate.

steps.title: Conclusion and mark points
steps.content:
Because the hydrogen ions react with the carbonate ions, the overall concentration of marine carbonate ions ($CO_3^{2-}$) decreases. 

To secure both marks on an IB exam, ensure you mention:
1. The formation of $H^+$ from carbonic acid.
2. The reaction between $H^+$ and $CO_3^{2-}$ forming $HCO_3^-$.

steps.title: Analyze global temperature trend
steps.content:
To describe the first trend, we look at the **Global Temperature Anomaly** column. We can see that the values increase every decade. 

In 1980, the anomaly was $+0.25 \text{ °C}$, and by 2020, it had risen to $+1.02 \text{ °C}$. This represents a steady and consistent warming trend over the 40-year period.

steps.title: Analyze atmospheric CO2 trend
steps.content:
Next, we describe the trend for **Atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$** separately. Looking at the data, the concentration of carbon dioxide increases without interruption.

In 1980, the concentration was $338 \text{ ppm}$, and it increased to $414 \text{ ppm}$ by 2020. This demonstrates a continuous rise in greenhouse gases in the atmosphere during this timeframe.

steps.title: Review syllabus connection
steps.content: These trends are central to **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**. Specifically, **D4.3.1** discusses the anthropogenic (human-caused) causes of climate change, such as the combustion of fossil fuels which releases $\text{CO}_2$ and leads to the global warming trend seen in the temperature data.

steps.title: Identify the shared trend
steps.content: To answer this question using an integrated approach, we first look at the overall direction of both sets of data. Between 1980 and 2020, both the average global temperature anomaly and the atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ concentration show a continuous upward trend. This means that as time progresses, both variables are increasing simultaneously.

steps.content: Finally, we integrate these observations to describe the relationship. The data shows that temperature and $\text{CO}_2$ levels rise together, indicating a strong positive correlation. This relationship is a key concept in **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**, where the increase in greenhouse gases is linked to the increase in global temperatures.

steps.title: Analyze the objective
steps.content: To describe the trends, we will use **Interval Progression Analysis**. This means we'll look at the data in 10-year steps to see if the changes are consistent. We need to describe what is happening to both the temperature anomaly and the $CO_2$ levels over the entire 40-year period.

highlights.text: Describe the trends in global temperature and CO2 levels from 1980 to 2020.

steps.title: Examine 1980 to 2000
steps.content:
Let's look at the first two decades:

*   **1980 to 1990:** Temperature increased by $0.15^{\circ}C$ ($0.40 - 0.25$) and $CO_2$ rose by $16\text{ ppm}$ ($354 - 338$).
*   **1990 to 2000:** Temperature increased by another $0.15^{\circ}C$ ($0.55 - 0.40$) and $CO_2$ rose by another $16\text{ ppm}$ ($370 - 354$).

In this first half, both variables are increasing at a very steady rate.

gdc.instructions: Perform simple subtractions for temperature and CO2 differences.

steps.title: Examine 2000 to 2020
steps.content:
Now let's look at the more recent decades:

*   **2000 to 2010:** Temperature increased by $0.17^{\circ}C$ ($0.72 - 0.55$) and $CO_2$ rose by $20\text{ ppm}$ ($390 - 370$).
*   **2010 to 2020:** Temperature increased by $0.30^{\circ}C$ ($1.02 - 0.72$) and $CO_2$ rose by $24\text{ ppm}$ ($414 - 390$).

Notice that the rate of increase for both temperature and $CO_2$ is actually accelerating in these later intervals.

steps.title: Summarize the overall trends
steps.content:
Since every single 10-year interval showed an increase, we can conclude:

1.  **Temperature Trend:** There is a steady and continuous increase in the global temperature anomaly, rising from $+0.25^{\circ}C$ in 1980 to $+1.02^{\circ}C$ in 2020. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **$CO_2$ Trend:** There is a continuous increase in atmospheric $CO_2$ levels, rising from $338\text{ ppm}$ in 1980 to $414\text{ ppm}$ in 2020. <marks>A1</marks>

This positive correlation is a key part of **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**. If you want to see how these $CO_2$ levels are tracked over even longer periods, check out the Keeling Curve in **Topic C4.2**.

steps.title: Identify CO2 as a greenhouse gas
steps.content: To explain the relationship, we first need to identify the role of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. $\text{CO}_2$ is a greenhouse gas. These gases play a crucial role in the Earth's energy balance because their molecular structure allows them to absorb and re-emit infrared radiation (heat). This concept is explored in **Topic D4.3: Climate change**.

steps.title: The mechanism of trapping heat
steps.content: The mechanism works like this: Short-wave solar radiation passes through the atmosphere and is absorbed by the Earth's surface. The Earth then re-emits this energy as long-wave infrared radiation (heat). Greenhouse gases like $\text{CO}_2$ trap this outgoing thermal radiation, preventing it from escaping into space. This is known as the greenhouse effect.

steps.title: Link concentration to temperature rise
steps.content:
As the concentration of $\text{CO}_2$ increases, the atmosphere becomes more efficient at trapping heat. Looking at the data, we see the concentration rose from $338\text{ ppm}$ in 1980 to $414\text{ ppm}$ in 2020. 

$$\text{Increase in } \text{CO}_2 = 414 - 338 = 76\text{ ppm}$$

This increase enhances the greenhouse effect, which directly causes the rise in the **average global temperature anomaly (°C)** observed in the table ($+0.25$ to $+1.02$).

gdc.instructions: Perform subtraction to find the total change in concentration and temperature.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the 1980 values from the 2020 values for both columns.

desmos.instructions: Enter the subtraction expressions to find the differences.

calculator.description: Calculate the total increase in CO2 and Temperature Anomaly over the 40-year period.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: In summary, there is a positive relationship because higher $\text{CO}_2$ levels lead to a more intense greenhouse effect. By trapping more thermal energy within the atmosphere, increasing $\text{CO}_2$ levels cause the global temperature to rise.

steps.title: Analyze the data trend
steps.content:
To understand the relationship, let's look at how the values change over time in the provided table. 

In 1980, the $\text{CO}_2$ level was $338\text{ ppm}$ and the temperature anomaly was $+0.25^\circ\text{C}$. By 2020, the $\text{CO}_2$ level rose to $414\text{ ppm}$ and the temperature anomaly reached $+1.02^\circ\text{C}$. 

We can see that as the years progress, both the atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ concentration and the global temperature anomaly show a continuous upward trend.

steps.title: Identify the positive correlation
steps.content:
When two variables increase together, we call this a **positive correlation**. 

From the data, we can state that there is a direct positive correlation between atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ levels and global temperature change: as $\text{CO}_2$ levels increase over time, the temperature anomaly also increases accordingly.

steps.title: Explain the biological mechanism
steps.content:
This correlation exists because $\text{CO}_2$ is a **greenhouse gas**. This concept is covered in **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**. 

Greenhouse gases trap long-wave radiation (heat) in the Earth's atmosphere, preventing it from escaping into space. As the concentration of these gases increases—primarily due to human activities like the combustion of fossil fuels (Topic C4.2)—the greenhouse effect is enhanced, leading to a rise in global temperatures.

steps.title: State the final relationship
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your explanation should connect the data to the theory:

1.  **Identify the role**: $\text{CO}_2$ is a greenhouse gas that traps heat in the atmosphere. <mark>R1</mark>
2.  **Link to the trend**: Increased $\text{CO}_2$ levels enhance the greenhouse effect, which causes the rising global temperature anomaly observed in the table. <mark>R1</mark>

highlights.text: Explain the relationship between atmospheric \text{CO}_2$ levels and global temperature change.

steps.title: Recognize the Trend
steps.content:
Before explaining the 'why', let's look at the 'what' in the data. Between 1980 and 2020, we can see a clear **positive correlation** between the two variables:

*   Atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ increased from $338\text{ ppm}$ to $414\text{ ppm}$ (an increase of $76\text{ ppm}$).
*   The global temperature anomaly rose from $+0.25\text{ °C}$ to $+1.02\text{ °C}$ (an increase of $0.77\text{ °C}$).

This trend suggests that as carbon dioxide levels rise, global temperatures also rise. This is a core concept in **Topic D4.3: Climate Change**.

steps.title: Define the Role of CO2
steps.content:
To earn the first mark, you must explain that **$\text{CO}_2$ is a greenhouse gas**. 

Greenhouse gases have a specific molecular structure that allows them to absorb **long-wave (infrared) radiation** that the Earth's surface emits after being warmed by the sun. By absorbing this radiation, $\text{CO}_2$ effectively traps heat within the atmosphere and prevents it from escaping into space.

steps.title: Explain the Energy Balance
steps.content:
To earn the second mark, link the trapping of heat to the temperature rise using the **Energy Balance Framework**:

1.  **Energy In vs. Energy Out**: For Earth's temperature to remain stable, the energy arriving from the sun must equal the energy escaping back into space.
2.  **Reduced Escape**: As we increase the concentration of $\text{CO}_2$, a larger proportion of the outgoing long-wave radiation is absorbed and re-emitted back toward the surface.
3.  **Warming**: This reduces the amount of radiation escaping into space, shifting the energy balance. This **enhanced greenhouse effect** results in a net accumulation of energy, leading to the increase in global temperatures observed in the table.

parts.content: (a) Explain how global warming has caused poleward and upslope range shifts in temperate species.
parts.markscheme:
- Increased mean temperatures reduce suitability of habitats at lower latitudes/altitudes for some temperate species <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individuals that can disperse colonize cooler areas poleward and/or at higher altitude <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Populations persist where conditions remain within their thermal tolerance/optimal performance range (maintaining preferred temperature niche) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Movement results in a shift of the species’ geographic range margins (leading edge expands, trailing edge contracts) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reference to associated changes such as altered phenology or biotic interactions (e.g. competition/predation) contributing to observed shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: (b) Describe why tropical montane species are particularly sensitive to temperature changes.
parts.markscheme:
- Many tropical montane species are adapted to narrow temperature ranges (specialists with small thermal tolerance) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temperatures vary relatively little seasonally in the tropics, so species have limited physiological acclimation capacity to warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Warming shifts suitable climate upslope, reducing available habitat area at higher elevations (“mountaintop effect”) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited dispersal opportunities as there may be no higher/cooler habitat available, increasing extinction risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sensitivity may be increased by dependence on specific microclimates/biotic interactions (e.g. host plants, pollinators) that also shift with temperature <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Small, isolated populations at high elevation may have low genetic diversity, reducing adaptive potential <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: (c) Explain how climate warming can affect species distributions and community composition in mountain ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- As temperatures rise, isotherms move upslope, shifting the climatic envelopes of many species to higher elevations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Upslope shifts can cause range contractions where mountaintops limit further movement, increasing local/global extinction risk <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Differential dispersal rates lead to novel species assemblages and changes in community composition <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Altered phenology (e.g. earlier flowering/breeding) can create mismatches between interacting species (pollinators–plants, predators–prey) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Changes in abiotic conditions (snowpack, soil moisture) modify habitat suitability and productivity, affecting which species can persist <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Warmer conditions can allow lowland species, pests, and pathogens to invade higher elevations, increasing competition/disease pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resulting changes can affect ecosystem functions (e.g. primary productivity, nutrient cycling) through loss/replacement of key functional groups <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Upward movement and loss of cold-adapted species can reduce beta diversity/endemism in high-elevation zones <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Credit other valid linked explanations of distribution/community change driven by warming in mountain systems <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the percentage of tropical studies that reported elevational shifts. Show working.
parts.markscheme:
* Total tropical studies $= 23$ (elevation) $+ 0$ (latitude) $= 23$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Substitute into percentage calculation: $\frac{\text{elevational tropical studies}}{\text{total tropical studies}} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Percentage $= \frac{23}{23} \times 100 = 100\%$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Using the pie charts, compare elevational and latitudinal shifts inside the tropics and outside the tropics.
parts.markscheme:
* Inside the tropics: elevational shifts only ($23$ studies) and no latitudinal shifts ($0$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This is therefore $100\%$ elevational and $0\%$ latitudinal inside the tropics <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Outside the tropics: both elevational ($137$) and latitudinal ($169$) shifts occur <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Latitudinal shifts are slightly more common than elevational shifts outside the tropics because $169 > 137$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content: Suggest one ecological explanation for why tropical species show only elevational shifts, while species outside the tropics show both elevational and latitudinal shifts.
parts.markscheme:
* In the tropics, temperature changes strongly with elevation (steep altitudinal temperature gradient), so species can track suitable temperatures by moving upslope <marks>1 mark</marks>
* In the tropics, temperature changes relatively little with latitude (shallow latitudinal temperature gradient near the equator), so moving poleward gives limited climatic benefit <marks>1 mark</marks>
_**OR**_
* Mountains provide nearby cooler habitats (microclimates) enabling upslope shifts, whereas an equivalent “cooler latitude” option may be far away or not available within equatorial regions <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.

questions.specification: Global warming can trigger feedback mechanisms that either amplify or reduce the initial temperature change.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D4.3 Climate change > D4.3.2 Positive feedback cycles in global warming
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D4.3.2 Positive feedback cycles in global warming
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for a definition of positive feedback and three examples of these cycles within the context of global warming, which directly aligns with topic D4.3.2.

parts.content: Outline what is meant by positive feedback in the context of climate change.
parts.markscheme:
- Positive feedback is a process in which an initial change causes effects that further increase that change in the same direction (amplifies the original change) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links the idea to global warming, e.g. warming leads to changes that cause additional warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Includes the idea of a self-reinforcing cycle/loop (not self-correcting) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mentions that the effect continues until a limiting factor intervenes or conditions change <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain three examples of positive feedback cycles that can amplify global warming.
parts.markscheme:
- Ice–albedo feedback: rising temperature melts snow/ice, decreasing albedo so more solar radiation is absorbed, causing further warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Permafrost feedback: warming thaws permafrost, releasing methane and/or carbon dioxide, enhancing the greenhouse effect and causing further warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Water vapour feedback: warming increases evaporation, increasing atmospheric water vapour (a greenhouse gas), strengthening the greenhouse effect and causing further warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ocean solubility feedback: warming reduces CO$_2$ solubility in oceans, so less CO$_2$ is absorbed (or more is released), increasing atmospheric CO$_2$ and warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Forest dieback feedback: heat/drought increases tree mortality or fire, reducing carbon sequestration and releasing stored CO$_2$, increasing atmospheric CO$_2$ and warming <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Other valid positive feedback example linked clearly to amplification of warming <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how positive feedback can lead to rapid or non-linear changes in climate systems and outline one possible consequence for ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- Positive feedback amplifies the initial warming so the rate of temperature increase can accelerate over time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Can push the climate system towards thresholds/tipping points where a small additional change produces a large response <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains a non-linear response (change is not proportional to the initial forcing) due to reinforcing loops <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links to at least one feedback mechanism contributing to accelerated change (e.g. ice loss, methane release, increased water vapour) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ecosystem consequence stated and linked to the rapid/non-linear change, e.g. loss of polar habitat, coral bleaching, altered species distributions, increased extinction risk, biome shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains how the consequence arises (e.g. temperature/precipitation changes exceeding tolerance or adaptation rate) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recognises reduced resilience/limited time for migration or adaptation <marks>1 mark</marks>
